methodsAttempted.query: site:emory.edu Emory Center for AI Learning generative AI faculty students policy syllabus
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search found the Emory Libraries AI teaching guide and a Rollins School of Public Health teaching-technology page with school-scope generative AI guidance.

Public Responsible AI pages linked data-security, content-generation, disclosure, communications, OIT, and undergraduate honor-code resources. The EASAT registry redirected to Microsoft sign-in and was treated as inaccessible.

artifacts.summary: Official-source discovery found Emory Responsible AI guidance, Emory College Learning Design AI guidance, an Emory College Honor Council PDF, and a Rollins School of Public Health teaching page. Claims are limited to public official pages and retain school-scope limits where the source is not university-wide. The QS ranking row was not used as policy evidence.

artifacts.claimText: Emory maintains a public Responsible AI site that presents Emory's Responsible AI Guiding Principles, developed through multiple rounds of input from across the Emory community, with an iterative process over time.

artifacts.claimText: Emory College Learning Design guidance cites Honor Council language stating that, where AI is not allowed, using an AI program to generate assignment content constitutes plagiarism and an Honor Code violation, and unauthorized AI use may also constitute unauthorized assistance or other Honor Code violations.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Using an artificial intelligence program to generate any content for any assignment in this course ... constitutes plagiarism and is a violation of the Honor Code. The use of an artificial intelligence program in this course without permission from the instructor may also constitute seeking unauthorized assistance or violate other provisions of the Honor Code.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Generative AI in Higher Education / suggested syllabus language when AI is not allowed
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.notes: Approved with the source's condition that the language applies when AI is not allowed or is used without instructor permission.

artifacts.claimText: The Emory College Honor Council AI information sheet says Emory does not currently license an AI detection program, detection tools can produce false positives or negatives, and detection results alone would not ordinarily be sufficient to find a student responsible for a violation.

artifacts.title: Emory University AI Policy Staged Findings
artifacts.summary: Staged findings for Emory University are based on public official Emory sources: Responsible AI guidance, Emory College Learning Design AI guidance, an Emory College Honor Council PDF, and Rollins School of Public Health teaching guidance. Claims cover public Responsible AI source status, sensitive-data handling with Emory-approved secure AI tools, EASAT review scope, course-context AI permissions, Emory College Honor Council academic-integrity language, AI detection limitations, and Rollins school-scope course-policy guidance.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
The secure AI registry redirected to Microsoft sign-in and was not accessed; claims about EASAT are limited to the public Responsible AI page text.
The Rollins claim is school-scoped and should not be read as a university-wide course policy.
No human review or canonical publication is asserted by this staged bundle.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
